findings.claim: Read across the log, ADR-0018, ADR-0020, ADR-0021, ADR-0031, and ADR-0039 (five Accepted records) each circle the same underlying, never-finally-closed question: can duckadrift structurally guarantee that a primitive (link parsing, path containment, external-reference classification, number-scoping, the check pipeline, status recognition) is implemented exactly once, so a fix never fails to reach a sibling consumer? Each record explicitly declares the problem 'closed at the primitive' or 'complete,' and each subsequent record in the set instead finds the identical duplication-drift pattern recurring in a new area, undercutting the prior closure claim rather than building past it. The checker assesses this as a five-record recurring-revision chain on one unresolved architectural discrimination, not five independent, separately-resolved bugs.

citations.quote: The five founding false positives are closed at the primitive, not the instance.

ADR-0018 consolidated three duplicated primitives after five false positives traced to
each being kept in more than one copy.

every markdown-parsing
and reference-resolution path in the tool is one implementation.

The founding false positives
(ADR-0018) and the scanner's leaks (ADR-0020) shared one shape — a primitive applied
correctly in one place and not another — and that shape is not found by waiting for a
reviewer to report an instance of it.

The multi-round treadmill of the recent tags — patch an instance, ship, find the same bug in the next
instance — ends here, not through more diligence but by removing the duplication diligence
was compensating for.

A second pipeline
is the parallel-primitive failure this repository has already shipped once and
now treats as a standing audit concern.

Two recognizers disagreeing about the same fact, with a third about to be
patched in, is the exact drift this tool exists to catch — found inside the tool, again.
Status is resolved by one shared primitive.

findings.consequence: If this reading holds, maintainers should not treat the 'closed at the primitive' / 'complete' / 'resolved by one shared primitive' language in ADR-0018, ADR-0020, ADR-0021, or ADR-0039 as evidence that duplicate-primitive drift is actually retired as a risk class, since ADR-0031 itself now frames it as a standing audit concern rather than a settled fact. A maintainer would likely want either a structural safeguard (a lint or architectural test that positively verifies single-sourcing of shared primitives across checks) or an explicit ADR acknowledging this as an accepted, ongoing residual risk rather than a repeatedly-declared-solved problem.
